definition: A creational design that ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global point of access to it, typically by controlling construction and exposing a single accessor.
ruleOfThumb: Use when exactly one instance with controlled lifecycle is required (e.g., a shared registry or coordinator). Prefer dependency injection for testability and flexible lifecycles instead of using a single global instance by default.

citation.author: Erich Gamma, Richard Helm, Ralph Johnson, John Vlissides
citation.bookTitle: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

questions.prompt: What is this class primarily implementing?

options.text: A factory that returns a new Config each time getInstance is called.
options.explanation: Incorrect. The code caches the created object in a static field and returns that same object on subsequent calls instead of creating new instances.

options.text: A module-level helper that only exposes static utility functions without instance state.
options.explanation: Wrong. There is instance state (settings) and a private constructor, so this is not just a static utilities holder.

options.text: A lazily created, single shared Config instance accessed through a static accessor.
options.explanation: Correct. Private constructor prevents external construction and the static accessor initializes and returns a single shared instance (lazy initialization).

options.text: An implementation of dependency injection for Config construction.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Dependency injection would accept and manage Config instances externally; here the class itself controls creation and lifetime.

questions.prompt: Consider a module that exports a single instantiated logger like below. Which statement best captures a practical downside of this approach compared to a controlled-access class?

options.text: This guarantees thread-safe lazy initialization and is always better for tests.
options.explanation: False. Exporting an eagerly created instance is simple but not lazy; it may introduce initialization-order issues. It also makes tests harder to isolate because substitution requires module mocking.

options.text: It's simpler, but it reduces control over lifecycle and makes substituting or mocking the instance harder in tests.
options.explanation: Correct. An exported instance is easy to use, but you lose control over when it's created and swapping it out requires replacing module exports (harder than injecting a mock). Controlled-access approaches allow delayed creation or explicit injection for tests.

options.text: Functionally equivalent to registering the logger with a DI container; nothing to worry about.
options.explanation: Not equivalent. A DI container provides explicit wiring and test-time substitution; an exported instance couples consumers to that concrete object and the module's initialization time.

options.text: Prefer this for mutable global state because it's always safer than creating instances per consumer.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Mutable global state can lead to hidden coupling and lifecycle issues; creating instances per consumer or using injected single shared services are safer depending on ownership requirements.

questions.prompt: Which version is generally preferable when you need a single, application-wide service but also want reasonable control over initialization and test substitution?

options.text: Version A is better because it centralizes and delays creation, letting you add controlled initialization or swap the instance for testing.
options.explanation: Version A is preferable in many cases. The accessor centralizes construction, enabling lazy initialization and a single point to add synchronization, logging, or a test hook such as a setInstance() for substitution. That extra control reduces initialization-order bugs and makes it easier to manage lifecycle across the app while still providing a single shared object.

options.text: Version B is better because eager module exports are always simpler and introduce no downsides.
options.explanation: Overly optimistic. While Version B is simple, eager export removes control over when creation happens (risking init-order problems), and swapping or mocking it requires module-level replacement which is harder and more brittle than providing a single controlled accessor. If you truly need a single shared service with predictable lifecycle and testability, the controlled accessor offers clearer trade-offs.
